name: Mock Test - 08
instruction: The Question Paper contains three sections. Section A has 25 questions. Attempt any 20 questions. Section B has 24 questions. Attempt any20 questions. Section C has 6 questions. Attempt any 5 questions. All questions carry equal marks. There is no negative marking.

questions.question_text: For a thin spherical shell of uniform surface charge density {tex}\sigma {/tex} , the magnitude of {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} at a distance r, when r > R (radius of shell) is
questions.question_html: For a thin spherical shell of uniform surface charge density {tex}\sigma {/tex} , the magnitude of {tex}\vec{E}{/tex}  at a distance r, when r > R (radius of shell) is
questions.explanation: {tex}E =\frac{4\pi R^2 σ}{4\pi \epsilon_0 r^2}{/tex}

questions.question_text: A 4 {tex}\mu{/tex} F capacitor is charged to 400 V. If its plates are joined through a resistance of 2k {tex}\Omega{/tex} then heat produced in the resistance is:
questions.question_html: A 4 {tex}\mu{/tex}F capacitor is charged to 400 V. If its plates are joined through a resistance of 2k{tex}\Omega{/tex} then heat produced in the resistance is:
questions.explanation: Heat produced in the 2 k {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistor = Energy stored in the charged capacitor {tex}=\frac{1}{2} C V^{2}=\frac{1}{2} \times 4 \times 10^{-6} \times(400)^{2} \mathrm{~J}{/tex} = 0.32 J

questions.question_text: In the circuit shown, if a conducting wire is connected between points B and D, the current in this wire will:
questions.question_html: In the circuit shown, if a conducting wire is connected between points B and D, the current in this wire will:

options.text: flow in the direction which will be decided by the value of V
options.html: flow in the direction which will be decided by the value of V

questions.explanation: Current through arm ABC, I = {tex}\frac{v}{4 \ + \ 4}=\frac{v}{8}{/tex} P.D. between A and B, V A - V B = {tex}\frac{V}{8} \times 4=\frac{V}{2}{/tex} Similarly, V A - V D = {tex}\frac{V}{1 \ + \ 3} \times 1=\frac{V}{4}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} (V A - V B )-(V A - V D ) = {tex}\frac{V}{2}-\frac{V}{4}=\frac{V}{4}{/tex} or V D - V B = {tex}\frac{V}{4}{/tex} It follows that V D > V B . Hence the current will flow from D to B in arm BD.

questions.question_text: A parallel plate capacitor is made of two dielectric blocks in series. One of the blocks has thickness d 1 and dielectric constant k 1 and the other has thickness d 2 and dielectric constant k 2 as shown in Fig. This arrangement can be thought of as a dielectric slab of thickness d (= d 1 +d 2 ) and effective dielectric constant k. Then k is
questions.question_html: A parallel plate capacitor is made of two dielectric blocks in series. One of the blocks has thickness d1 and dielectric constant k1 and the other has thickness d2 and dielectric constant k2 as shown in Fig. This arrangement can be thought of as a dielectric slab of thickness d (= d1+d2) and effective dielectric constant k. Then k is
questions.explanation: Capacitance of a parallel plate capacitor filled with dielectric of constant k 1 and thickness d 1 is {tex}C_{1}=\frac{k_{1} \varepsilon_{0} A}{d_{1}}{/tex} Similarly for other, {tex}C_{2}=\frac{k_{2} \varepsilon_{0} A}{d_{2}}{/tex} ,having dielectric of constant k 2 and thickness d 2 Both capacitors are in series so equivalent capacitance C is related as: {tex}\frac{1}{C}=\frac{1}{C_{1}}+\frac{1}{C_{2}}=\frac{d_{1}}{k_{1} \varepsilon_{0} A}+\frac{d_{2}}{K_{2} \varepsilon_{0} A}=\frac{1}{\varepsilon_{0} A}\left[\frac{k_{2} d_{1}+k_{1} d_{2}}{k_{1} k_{2}}\right]{/tex} So {tex}C=\frac{k_{1} k_{2} \varepsilon_{0} A}{\left(k_{1} d_{2}+k_{2} d_{1}\right)}{/tex} ...(i) {tex}C^{\prime}=\frac{k \varepsilon_{0} A}{d}{/tex} ...(ii) Where d = (d 1 +d 2 ) So, Multiply the numerator and denominator of Eqn. i with (d 1 +d 2 ), {tex}C=\frac{k_{1} k_{2} \varepsilon_{0} A}{\left(k_{1} d_{2}+k_{2} d_{1}\right)} \cdot \frac{\left(d_{1}+d_{2}\right)}{\left(d_{1}+d_{2}\right)}=\frac{k_{1} k_{2}\left(d_{1}+d_{2}\right)}{\left(k_{1} d_{2}+k_{2} d_{1}\right)} \cdot \frac{\varepsilon_{0} A}{\left(d_{1}+d_{2}\right)}{/tex} ...(iii) Comparing Eq. II and III, the dielectric constant of the new capacitor is: {tex}k=\frac{k_{1} k_{2}\left(d_{1}+d_{2}\right)}{\left(k_{1} d_{2}+k_{2} d_{1}\right)}{/tex}

questions.question_text: The ratio of masses of three wires is 1 : 2 : 3 and that of their lengths is 3 : 2 : 1. If the wires are made of same material, the ratio of their resistance will be:
questions.question_html: The ratio of masses of three wires is 1 : 2 : 3 and that of their lengths is 3 : 2 : 1. If the wires are made of same material, the ratio of their resistance will be:
questions.explanation: Mass = Volume {tex}\times{/tex} density = AId or A = {tex}\frac{m}{i d}{/tex} {tex}\therefore R=\rho \frac{l}{A}=\rho \frac{l}{\frac m{ l d}}=\rho \frac{l^{2} d}{m}{/tex} i.e., {tex}R \propto \frac{l^{2}}{m}{/tex} Given m 1 : m 2 : m 3 = 1 : 2 : 3 and l : l : l = 3 : 2 : 1 {tex}\therefore R_{1}: R_{2} : R_{3}=\frac{3^{2}}{1}: \frac{2^{2}}{2}: \frac{1^{2}}{3}=9: 2: \frac{1}{3}{/tex} = 27 : 6 : 1

questions.question_text: It is observed in a potentiometer experiment that no current passes through the galvanometer, when the terminals of the cell are connected across a certain length of the potentiometer wire. On shunting the cell by a 2 Ω resistance, the balancing length is reduced to half. The internal resistance of the cell is
questions.question_html: It is observed in a potentiometer experiment that no current passes through the galvanometer, when the terminals of the cell are connected across a certain length of the potentiometer wire. On shunting the cell by a 2 Ω resistance, the balancing length is reduced to half. The internal resistance of the cell is
questions.explanation: The internal resistance of the cell is given by: {tex}{r = \left( {\frac{{{I_1}}}{{{I_2}}} - 1} \right) \times R}{/tex} {tex}{r = \left( {\frac{{2{I_1}}}{{{I_1}}} - 1} \right) \times R}{/tex} {tex}{ \Rightarrow r = \left( {2 - 1} \right) \times R}{/tex} {tex}{r = R = 2\Omega }{/tex}

questions.question_text: When current changes from +2A to -2A in 0.05 sec, an emf of 8V is induced in a coil. The coefficient of self inductance of the coil is:
questions.question_html: When current changes from +2A to -2A in 0.05 sec, an emf of 8V is induced in a coil. The coefficient of self inductance of the coil is:
questions.explanation: {tex}L = - \frac{e}{{\frac{{\Delta i}}{{\Delta t}}}}{/tex} {tex}{{\Delta i} \over {\Delta t}} = - {4 \over {0.05}} = - 80{/tex} e = 8 volt {tex}{\rm{L}} = - {8 \over { - 80}} = 0.1{\rm{H}}{/tex}

questions.question_text: The intensity of magnetic field is H and the moment of a magnet is M Maximum potential energy is:
questions.question_html: The intensity of magnetic field is H and the moment of a magnet is M Maximum potential energy is:
questions.explanation: MH

questions.question_text: A loop of wire enclosing an area A is placed in a region where the magnetic field is perpendicular to the plane of the loop. The magnitude of B varies in time according to the expression B = {tex}{{\rm{B}}_{{\rm{max}}}}{{\rm{e}}^{ - {\rm{at}}}}{/tex} , where a is some constant. That is, at {tex}{\rm{t }} = {\rm{ }}0{/tex} the field is {tex}{{\rm{B}}_{{\rm{max}}}}{/tex} , and for {tex}{\rm{t }} > {\rm{ }}0{/tex} , the field decreases exponentially. Induced emf in the loop as a function of time is
questions.question_html: A loop of wire enclosing an area A is placed in a region where the magnetic field is perpendicular to the plane of the loop. The magnitude of B varies in time according to the expression B = {tex}{{\rm{B}}_{{\rm{max}}}}{{\rm{e}}^{ - {\rm{at}}}}{/tex}, where a is some constant. That is, at {tex}{\rm{t }} = {\rm{ }}0{/tex} the field is {tex}{{\rm{B}}_{{\rm{max}}}}{/tex}, and for {tex}{\rm{t }} > {\rm{ }}0{/tex}, the field decreases exponentially. Induced emf in the loop as a function of time is
questions.explanation: {tex}\left| e \right| = \frac{{d\phi }}{{dt}}{/tex} {tex}\phi = BA{/tex} {tex}\left| e \right| = \frac{{d\phi }}{{dt}} = \frac{{d\left( {BA} \right)}}{{dt}} = \frac{{d\left( {A{B_{\max }}{e^{ - at}}} \right)}}{{dt}} = - A{B_{\max }}\frac{d}{{dt}}{e^{ - at}}{/tex} {tex}\left| e \right| = aA{B_{\max }}{e^{ - at}}{/tex}

questions.question_text: Reactance of a 3 H inductor at a frequency of 80.0 Hz is
questions.question_html: Reactance of a 3 H inductor at a frequency of 80.0 Hz is
questions.explanation: L = 3 H, f = 80 Hz Inductive reactance, {tex}{X_L} = \omega L = 2\pi fL = 2 \times 3.14 \times 80 \times 3 = 1508\Omega {/tex}

questions.question_text: The resistance of an ammeter is 13 {tex}\Omega{/tex} and its scale is graduated for a current up to 100 A. After an additional shunt has been connected to this ammeter, it becomes possible to measure currents upto 750 A by this meter. The value of shunt resistance is:
questions.question_html: The resistance of an ammeter is 13 {tex}\Omega{/tex} and its scale is graduated for a current up to 100 A. After an additional shunt has been connected to this ammeter, it becomes possible to measure currents upto 750 A by this meter. The value of shunt resistance is:
questions.explanation: The original ammeter can be treated as the galvanometer. {tex}\therefore S=\frac{I_{g} G}{I-I_{g}}=\frac{100 \times 13}{750-100}=\frac{1300}{650}=2 \Omega{/tex}

questions.question_text: A potentiometer wire, 10 m long, has resistance 40 ohms. It is put in series with a resistance 760 ohms and connected to a 2 volt battery. The potential gradient in the wire is:
questions.question_html: A potentiometer wire, 10 m long, has resistance 40 ohms. It is put in series with a resistance 760 ohms and connected to a 2 volt battery. The potential gradient in the wire is:
questions.explanation: The total resistance is the sum of the resistance of the potentiometer and the external resistance. {tex}R = {R_{pot}} + {R_{ext}} = 40 + 760 = 800\Omega{/tex} Current through the potentiometer is {tex}I = \frac{E}{R} = \frac{2}{{800}}{/tex} {tex}I = 2.5 \times {10^{ - 3}}A{/tex} The potential drop across the potentiometer {tex}V = I \times {R_{pot}}{/tex} {tex}V = \left( {2.5 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}} \right) \times 40{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow V = 0.1\;V{/tex} The potential gradient = (potential drop across the potentiometer) / (length of the potentiometer wire) {tex} = \frac{{0.1}}{{10}}V/m{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} Potential gradient {tex} = 1 \times {10^{ - 2}}V/m{/tex}

questions.question_text: There are two coils A and B as shown in Figure. A current starts flowing in B as shown when A is moved towards B and stops when A stops moving. The current in A is counterclockwise. B is kept stationary when A moves. We can infer that
questions.question_html: There are two coils A and B as shown in Figure. A current starts flowing in B as shown when A is moved towards B and stops when A stops moving. The current in A is counterclockwise. B is kept stationary when A moves. We can infer that

options.text: there is a constant current in the clockwise direction in A
options.html: there is a constant current in the clockwise direction in A

options.text: there is no current in A
options.html: there is no current in A

options.text: there is a constant current in the counterclockwise direction in A
options.html: there is a constant current in the counterclockwise direction in A

options.text: there is a varying current in A
options.html: there is a varying current in A

questions.correct_option_text: there is a constant current in the counterclockwise direction in A
questions.explanation: Coil A must be carrying a constant current in counter-clockwise direction. When coil A moves towards coil B with constant velocity so the rate of change of magnetic flux due to coil B in coil A will be constant gives constant current in A in the same direction as in B by Lenz's law.

questions.question_text: A transformer is used to light a 100 W and 110 V lamp from a 220 V mains. If the main current is 0.5 A, the efficiency of the transformer is approximate:
questions.question_html: A transformer is used to light a 100 W and 110 V lamp from a 220 V mains. If the main current is 0.5 A, the efficiency of the transformer is approximate:
questions.explanation: The efficiency of a transformer {tex}\eta=\frac{\text { Output power }}{\text { Input power }}=\frac{V_{s} I_{s}}{V_{p} I_{p}}{/tex} Here V s I s = 100 W, V P = 220 V, I P = 0.5 A {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\eta=\frac{100}{220 \times 0.5}{/tex} = 0.90 = 90 %

questions.question_text: An electric charge 10 -3 {tex}\mu{/tex} C is placed at the origin (0, 0) of the (x-y) coordinate system. Two points A and B are situated at ( {tex}\sqrt{2}, \sqrt{2}){/tex} and (2, 0) respectively. The potential difference between points A and B will be
questions.question_html: An electric charge 10-3 {tex}\mu{/tex} C is placed at the origin (0, 0) of the (x-y) coordinate system. Two points A and B are situated at ({tex}\sqrt{2}, \sqrt{2}){/tex} and (2, 0) respectively. The potential difference between points A and B will be
questions.explanation: OA = {tex}\sqrt{(\sqrt{2}-0)^{2}+(\sqrt{2}-0)^{2}}{/tex} = 2 OB = {tex}\sqrt{(2-0)^{2}+(0-0)^{2}}{/tex} = 2 {tex}\therefore{/tex} OA = OB

questions.question_text: In the figure, a particle having mass 1 m = 5 g and charge q = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 C starts from rest at point a and moves in a straight line to point b. What is its speed v at point b?
questions.question_html:
In the figure, a particle having mass 1 m = 5 g and charge q = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9 C starts from rest at point a and

moves in a straight line to point b. What is its speed v at point b?
questions.explanation: {tex}V_{a}=9 \times 10^{9}\left[\frac{3 \times 10^{-9}}{0.01}+\frac{-3 \times 10^{-9}}{0.02}\right]{/tex} = 1350 V {tex}V_{b}=9 \times 10^{9}\left[\frac{3 \times 10^{-9}}{0.02}+\frac{-3 \times 10^{-9}}{0.01}\right]{/tex} = -1350 V V a - V b = 2700 V {tex}\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}=q\left(V_{a}-V_{b}\right){/tex} {tex}v=\sqrt{\frac{2 q\left(V_{a}-V_{b}\right)}{m}}=\sqrt{\frac{2 \times 2 \times 10^{-9} \times 2700}{5 \times 10^{-3}}}{/tex} = 4.65 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 ms -1 = 4.65 cms - 1

questions.question_text: A proton (charge +e) enters a magnetic field of strength B (Tesla) with speed v, parallel to the direction of magnetic lines of force. The force on the proton is
questions.question_html: A proton (charge +e) enters a magnetic field of strength B (Tesla) with speed v, parallel to the direction of magnetic lines of force. The force on the proton is
questions.explanation: Lorentz force is given by {tex}F = Bqv\sin \theta {/tex} When the proton enters the magnetic field parallel to the direction of the lines of force, {tex}\theta = 0{/tex} . Therefore, F = 0

questions.question_text: Angular resonant frequency and Q-factor of a series LCR circuit with L = 3.0 H, C = 27 {tex}\mu {\rm{F}}{/tex} , and R = 7.4 {tex}\Omega {/tex} are respectively
questions.question_html: Angular resonant frequency and Q-factor of a series LCR circuit with L = 3.0 H, C = 27 {tex}\mu {\rm{F}}{/tex}, and R = 7.4 {tex}\Omega {/tex} are respectively
questions.explanation: L = 3.0 H C = 27 {tex}\mu {\rm{F}}{/tex} R = 7.4 {tex}\Omega {/tex} Angular resonant frequency, {tex}\omega = {1 \over {\sqrt {LC} }} = {1 \over {\sqrt {3 \times 27 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}} }} = {1 \over {9 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}} = 111rad/s{/tex} Q-factor, {tex}Q = {1 \over R}\sqrt {{L \over C}} = {1 \over {7.4}}\sqrt {{3 \over {27 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}}}} = 45{/tex}

questions.question_text: A bar magnet of magnetic moment 1.5 J/T lies parallel to the direction of a uniform magnetic field of 0.22 T. What is the torque acting on it?
questions.question_html: A bar magnet of magnetic moment 1.5 J/T lies parallel to the direction of a uniform magnetic field of 0.22 T. What is the torque acting on it?
questions.explanation: Torque {tex}=mBsin\theta{/tex} Since magnetic moment and magnetic field are parallel, angle between them is zero. Hence torque acting is zero.

questions.question_text: The equivalent resistance of two resistances P and Q which are in parallel is
questions.question_html: The equivalent resistance of two resistances P and Q which are in parallel is
questions.explanation: The reciprocal of the equivalent resistance of two resistances connected in parallel is equal to the sum of the reciprocals of the two resistances. {tex}\frac{1}{{{R_{eq}}}} = \frac{1}{P} + \frac{1}{Q}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {R_{eq}} = \frac{{PQ}}{{P + Q}}{/tex}

questions.question_text: Two identical charged spheres suspended from a common point by two massless strings of lengths l, are initially at a distance d(d << l) apart because of their mutual repulsion. The charges begin to leak from both the spheres at a constant rate. As a result, the spheres approach each other with a velocity v. Then v varies as a function of the distance x between the spheres, as
questions.question_html: Two identical charged spheres suspended from a common point by two massless strings of lengths l, are initially at a distance d(d << l) apart because of their mutual repulsion. The charges begin to leak from both the spheres at a constant rate. As a result, the spheres approach each other with a velocity v. Then v varies as a function of the distance x between the spheres, as
questions.explanation: From {tex}\triangle{/tex} ACO of forces, {tex}\frac{F}{A C}=\frac{m g}{O C}{/tex} {tex}\frac{k q^{2}}{x^{2}(\frac x 2)}=\frac{m g}{\sqrt{l^{2}-\left(\frac{x}{2}\right)^{2}}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{2 k q^{2}}{x^{3}}=\frac{m g}{l}{/tex} [ {tex}\frac{x}{2}{/tex} << l] {tex}\therefore{/tex} q 2 = {tex}\frac{m g}{2 k l}\cdot{/tex} x 3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} q 2 {tex}\propto{/tex} x 3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} q {tex}\propto{/tex} x 3/2 {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d q}{d t} \propto \frac{3}{2} x^{\frac{1}{2}} \frac{d x}{d t}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d q}{d t} \propto \frac{3}{2} x^{\frac{1}{2}} \cdot v{/tex} As {tex}\frac{d q}{d t}{/tex} is constant for both spheres, so {tex}v \propto \frac{1}{x^{\frac{1}{2}}} \Rightarrow v \propto x^{-\frac{1}{2}}{/tex}

questions.question_text: Phase difference between voltage and current in a capacitor in ac circuit is
questions.question_html: Phase difference between voltage and current in a capacitor in ac circuit is
questions.explanation: An ac voltage v = v m sinωt applied to a capacitor drives a current in the capacitor i = i m sin (ωt + {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} ). Thus, the current through the capacitor is {tex}\frac{\pi }{2}{/tex} ahead of the applied voltage.

questions.question_text: Two coils of self-inductance 2 mH and 8 mH are placed so close together that the effective flux in one coil is completely linked with the other. The mutual inductance between these coils is
questions.question_html: Two coils of self-inductance 2 mH and 8 mH are placed so close together that the effective flux in one coil is completely linked with the other. The mutual inductance between these coils is
questions.explanation: {tex}M=K \sqrt{L_{1} L_{2}}{/tex} As the complete flux of one coil is linked with the other coil, so K = 1 {tex}\therefore M=\sqrt{L_{1} L_{2}}=\sqrt{2 \times 8}{/tex} = 4 mH

questions.question_text: The susceptibility of a magnetic substance is found to depend on temperature and the strength of the magnetising field. The material is a:
questions.question_html: The susceptibility of a magnetic substance is found to depend on temperature and the strength of the magnetising field. The material is a:
questions.explanation: The susceptibility of a paramagnetic substance depends both on the temperature and strength of the magnetising field.

questions.question_text: For measuring microampere( {tex}\mu{/tex} A) current, the galvanometer is
questions.question_html: For measuring microampere({tex}\mu{/tex}A) current, the galvanometer is
questions.explanation: For measurement of current of any magnitude, the galvanometer is always connected in series. This is to ensure that the entire current flows through the galvanometer. If the galvanometer were connected in parallel, the current branches out and only a part of the current will flow through it.

questions.question_text: To produce a magnetic field of {tex}\pi{/tex} tesla at the centre of circular loop of diameter 1 m, the current flowing through loop is:
questions.question_html: To produce a magnetic field of {tex}\pi{/tex} tesla at the centre of circular loop of diameter 1 m, the current flowing through loop is:
questions.explanation: As {tex}B=\frac{\mu_{0} N I}{2 a}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \pi=\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 1 \times I}{2 \times 0.5}{/tex} or I = 2.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 6 A

questions.question_text: Current density of a conductor is
questions.question_html: Current density of a conductor is

options.text: the net charge flowing through the area per unit time
options.html: the net charge flowing through the area per unit time

options.text: measure of the flow of electric charge in amperes per unit area of cross-section
options.html: measure of the flow of electric charge in amperes per unit area of cross-section

options.text: the net charge flowing through the area
options.html: the net charge flowing through the area

questions.correct_option_text: measure of the flow of electric charge in amperes per unit area of cross-section
questions.explanation: Current density J = {tex}\frac{I}{A}{/tex} In electromagnetism, current density is the electric current per unit area of cross section. Or It is the measure of the flow of electric charge in amperes per unit area of cross-section .It is a vector and has a direction along the area vector.

questions.question_text: Four-point charges -Q, -q, 2q, and 2Q are placed, one at each corner of the square. The relation between Q and q for which the potential at the centre of the square is zero is:
questions.question_html: Four-point charges -Q, -q, 2q, and 2Q are placed, one at each corner of the square. The relation between Q and q for which the potential at the centre of the square is zero is:
questions.explanation: Potential at the centre O is {tex}V=k\left[\frac{-Q}{a / \sqrt{2}}+\frac{-q}{a / \sqrt{2}}+\frac{2 q}{a / \sqrt{2}}+\frac{2 Q}{a / \sqrt{2}}\right]=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Q - q + 2q + 2Q = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Q + q = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Q = -q

questions.question_text: The phase difference between the current and voltage of LCR circuit in series combination at resonance is:
questions.question_html: The phase difference between the current and voltage of LCR circuit in series combination at resonance is:
questions.explanation: In an LCR resonance circuit, maximum current is limited by value of resistance; source voltage and circuit current are in phase. In such case, phase angle between voltage and current of a series resonance circuit is a function of frequency for fixed supply voltage which is zero. At resonant frequency V, I and VR are all in phase with each other At resonance LCR series circuit behaves as pure resistive circuit. For resistive circuit {tex}\phi{/tex} = 0 o .

questions.question_text: While keeping the area of cross-section of a solenoid the same, the number of turns and length of the solenoid are both doubled. The self-inductance of the coil will be:
questions.question_html: While keeping the area of cross-section of a solenoid the same, the number of turns and length of the solenoid are both doubled. The self-inductance of the coil will be:
questions.explanation: {tex}L=\frac{\mu_{0} N^{2} A}{l}{/tex} {tex}L^{\prime}=\frac{\mu_{0}(2 N)^{2} A}{2 l}=2 L{/tex}

questions.question_text: A bar magnet of magnetic moment 1.5 J/T lies aligned with the direction of a uniform magnetic field of 0.22 T. What is the amount of work required by an external torque to turn the magnet so as to align its magnetic moment opposite to the field direction?
questions.question_html: A bar magnet of magnetic moment 1.5 J/T lies aligned with the direction of a uniform magnetic field of 0.22 T. What is the amount of work required by an external torque to turn the magnet so as to align its magnetic moment opposite to the field direction?
questions.explanation: {tex}W=mB[cos\theta_{1}-cos\theta_{2}]=mB[cos0-cos\pi]{/tex} {tex}=2mB=2\times 1.5\times 0.22=0.66 J{/tex}

questions.question_text: A long solenoid of diameter 0.1 m has 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 4 turns per metre. At the centre of the solenoid, a coil of 100 turns and radius 0.01 m is placed with its axis coinciding with the solenoid axis. The current in the solenoid reduces at a constant rate to 0 A from 4 A in 0.05 s. If the resistance of the coil is 10 {tex}\pi^2 \Omega{/tex} , the total charge flowing through the coil during this time is:
questions.question_html: A long solenoid of diameter 0.1 m has 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 104 turns per metre. At the centre of the solenoid, a coil of 100 turns and radius 0.01 m is placed with its axis coinciding with the solenoid axis. The current in the solenoid reduces at a constant rate to 0 A from 4 A in 0.05 s. If the resistance of the coil is 10 {tex}\pi^2 \Omega{/tex}, the total charge flowing through the coil during this time is:
questions.explanation: {tex}q=\frac{\Delta \phi}{R}=\frac{\Delta(N B A)}{R} \equiv \frac{N A \Delta B}{R}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{N A \Delta\left(\mu_{0} n I\right)}{R}{/tex} or {tex}q=\frac{N \times \pi r^{2} \times \mu_{0} n \Delta I}{R}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{100 \times \pi(0.01)^{2} \times 4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 2 \times 10^{4} \times(4-0)}{10 \pi^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=32 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{C}=32 \mu \mathrm{C}{/tex}

questions.question_text: A current of 2 A flows through a 2 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistor when connected across a battery. The same battery supplies a current of 0.5 A when connected across a 9 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistor. The internal resistance of the battery is:
questions.question_html: A current of 2 A flows through a 2 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistor when connected across a battery. The same battery supplies a current of 0.5 A when connected across a 9 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistor. The internal resistance of the battery is:
questions.explanation: Let r {tex}\Omega{/tex} be the internal resistance of the battery In first case: {tex}\frac{\varepsilon}{r \ + \ 2}{/tex} = 2A In second case: {tex}\frac{\varepsilon}{r \ + \ 9}{/tex} = 0.5 A {tex}\therefore \frac{r \ + \ 9}{r \ + \ 2}=4 \text { or } r=\frac{1}{3} \Omega{/tex}

questions.question_text: The specific resistance of a conductor increases with:
questions.question_html: The specific resistance of a conductor increases with:
questions.explanation: The specific resistance of a conductor increases with the increase in temperature.

questions.question_text: The primary winding of a transformer has 500 turns, whereas its secondary has 5,000 turns. The primary is connected to an a.c. supply 20 V-50 Hz. The secondary will have an output of:
questions.question_html: The primary winding of a transformer has 500 turns, whereas its secondary has 5,000 turns. The primary is connected to an a.c. supply 20 V-50 Hz. The secondary will have an output of:
questions.explanation: {tex}\varepsilon_{s}=\frac{N_{s}}{N} \cdot \varepsilon_{p}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{5000}{500} \times 20=200 \mathrm{~V}{/tex} frequency remains the same.

questions.question_text: If the current is halved in a coil, then the energy stored is how much times the previous value?
questions.question_html: If the current is halved in a coil, then the energy stored is how much times the previous value?
questions.explanation: {tex}E = \frac{1}{2}L{i^2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{{E_1}}}{{{E_2}}} = {\left( {\frac{{{i_1}}}{{{i_2}}}} \right)^2}{/tex} Now, {tex}{i_2} = \frac{{{i_1}}}{2}{/tex} So, {tex}\frac{{{E_1}}}{{{E_2}}} = {\left( {\frac{{{i_1}}}{{\frac{{{i_1}}}{2}}}} \right)^2} = 4{/tex} Thus, {tex}{E_2} = \frac{{{E_1}}}{4}{/tex} thus the energy is 1/4 th of previous ones.

options.text: The number of charges pumped by the source from lower to higher potential energy

options.text: The work done per unit charge by the source in taking the charge from higher to lower potential energy

questions.correct_option_text: The work done per unit charge by the source in taking the charge from lower to higher potential energy
questions.explanation: An electric field exists in the electrolyte between the positive and negative terminals of the battery. In the external circuit, the current flows from the positive electrode to the negative electrode. To maintain continuity, in the electrolyte, the current (positive charges) flow from the negative electrode (lower potential) to the positive electrode (higher potential). Work done by the source in taking unit positive charge from lower to higher potential is called electromotive force.

questions.question_text: A potentiometer wire is 100 cm long and a constant potential difference is maintained across it. Two cells are connected in series first to support one another and then in opposite directions. The balance points are obtained at 50 cm and 10 cm from the positive end of the wire in the two cases. The ratio of emfs is:
questions.question_html: A potentiometer wire is 100 cm long and a constant potential difference is maintained across it. Two cells are connected in series first to support one another and then in opposite directions. The balance points are obtained at 50 cm and 10 cm from the positive end of the wire in the two cases. The ratio of emfs is:
questions.explanation: Lek k be the potential gradient in {tex}\frac V{cm}{/tex} . Then {tex}\varepsilon_{1}+\varepsilon_{2}{/tex} = 50 k {tex}\varepsilon_{1}-\varepsilon_{2}{/tex} = 10 k {tex}\therefore \varepsilon_{1}{/tex} = 30 k and {tex}\varepsilon_{2}{/tex} = 20 k {tex}\frac{\varepsilon_{1}}{\varepsilon_{2}}=\frac{3}{2}=3: 2{/tex}

questions.question_text: What uniform magnetic field applied perpendicular to a beam of electrons moving at 1.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 6 m/s, is required to make the electrons travel in a circular arc of radius 0.35 m?
questions.question_html: What uniform magnetic field applied perpendicular to a beam of electrons moving at 1.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 106 m/s, is required to make the electrons travel in a circular arc of radius 0.35 m?
questions.explanation: From {tex}\mathbf{r}=\frac{\mathbf{m v}}{\mathbf{q B}}{/tex} we find {tex}\mathbf{B}=\frac{\mathbf{m}_{\mathrm{e}} \mathbf{v}}{\mathrm{er}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\left(9.11 \times 10^{-31} \mathrm{~kg}\right)\left(1.30 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}\right)}{\left(1.60 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}\right)(0.350 \mathrm{~m})}{/tex} = 2.11 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -5 T.

questions.question_text: A charged particle (charge q) is moving in a circle of radius R with uniform speed v. The associated magnetic moment p is given by:
questions.question_html: A charged particle (charge q) is moving in a circle of radius R with uniform speed v. The associated magnetic moment p is given by:
questions.explanation: Here T = {tex}\frac{2 \pi R}{v}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{q}{T}=\frac{q v}{2 \pi R}{/tex} Magnetic moment, {tex}\mu=I A=\frac{q v}{2 \pi R} \times \pi R^{2}=\frac{q v R}{2}{/tex}

questions.question_text: Assertion (A): Positive charge always moves from a higher potential point to a lower potential point. Reason (R): Electric potential is a vector quantity.
questions.question_html:
Assertion (A): Positive charge always moves from a higher potential point to a lower potential point.
Reason (R): Electric potential is a vector quantity.

options.text: A is false but R is true.
options.html: A is false but R is true.

options.text: Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.

options.text: A is true but R is false.
options.html: A is true but R is false.

questions.correct_option_text: A is true but R is false.
questions.explanation: If two points P and Q in an electric field are separated by an infinitesimal distance {tex}\Delta{/tex} x and have a potential difference {tex}\Delta{/tex} V between them, E = {tex}-\Delta V \over \Delta x{/tex} . Here, negative sign implies that {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} has got a direction opposite to the potential gradient, i . e, in the direction of {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} , the potential decreases, i. e, positive charge always moves from a higher potential point to a lower potential point.

questions.explanation: If a material contains polar molecules, they will generally be in random orientations when no electric field is applied. An applied electric field will polarize the material by orienting the dipole moment of polar molecules.

questions.question_text: A charged cloud system produces an electric field in the air near the earth's surface. A particle of charge -2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 C is acted on by a downward electrostatic force of 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 N when placed in this field. The gravitational and electrostatic force, respectively, exerted on a proton placed in this field are
questions.question_html: A charged cloud system produces an electric field in the air near the earth's surface. A particle of charge -2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9 C is acted on by a downward electrostatic force of 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 N when placed in this field. The gravitational and electrostatic force, respectively, exerted on a proton placed in this field are
questions.explanation: E = {tex}\frac{F}{q}=\frac{3 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~N}}{2 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{C}}{/tex} = 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 3 NC -1 Electrostatic force on a proton = eE = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 3 = 2.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -16 N Gravitational force on a proton = mg = 1.67 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -27 kg {tex}\times{/tex} 9.8 ms -2 = 1.64 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -26 N
